mission.title: Install and Configure Kubearmor on Kubernetes
mission.description: KubeArmor is a cloud-native runtime security enforcement system that helps harden workloads by restricting their behavior at the system level using Linux security modules. Installing KubeArmor allows you to implement least-permissive policies and enhance the security of your Kubernetes environment.

steps.title: Add the KubeArmor Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the KubeArmor Helm repository to your local Helm configuration:
```bash
helm repo add kubearmor https://kubearmor.github.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install KubeArmor using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install KubeArmor with the following command, specifying the desired namespace (e.g., 'kubearmor'):
```bash
helm install kubearmor kubearmor/kubearmor --namespace kubearmor --create-namespace --version v1.6.9
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the KubeArmor pods are running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kubearmor
```

steps.title: Set up RBAC for KubeArmor
steps.description:
Create a ClusterRole and ClusterRoleBinding to allow KubeArmor to access necessary resources:
```bash
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: kubearmor-role
rules:
  - apiGroups: ["*"]
    resources: ["pods", "pods/log", "namespaces"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
---
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
metadata:
  name: kubearmor-role-binding
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: kubearmor-role
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: default
    namespace: kubearmor
EOF
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of KubeArmor will have the pods running in the specified namespace, and you will have configured resource limits and RBAC permissions. You can verify the setup by checking the pod status and ensuring they are in the 'Running' state.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kubearmor https://kubearmor.github.io/charts
helm repo update
helm install kubearmor kubearmor/kubearmor --namespace kubearmor --create-namespace --version v1.6.9

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: 500m
    memory: 512Mi
  requests:
    cpu: 250m
    memory: 256Mi

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the KubeArmor release from the cluster using Helm. This will remove all associated resources created by the Helm chart.
```bash
helm uninstall kubearmor --namespace kubearmor
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) that were installed with KubeArmor. This step is important to ensure that no leftover resources remain in the cluster.
```bash
kubectl delete crd kubearmorpolicies.kubearmor.io
kubectl delete crd kubearmorhosts.kubearmor.io
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the KubeArmor namespace to clean up all remaining resources. Verify that the namespace has been removed successfully.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kubearmor
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's a good practice to back up the current configuration and state of KubeArmor. You can export the current deployment and any relevant configurations.
```bash
kubectl get deployment kubearmor --namespace kubearmor -o yaml > kubearmor-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository to ensure you have the latest charts, then upgrade KubeArmor to the desired version (e.g., v1.7.0).
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade kubearmor kubearmor/kubearmor --namespace kubearmor --version v1.7.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, check the status of the KubeArmor pods to ensure they are running the new version correctly.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kubearmor
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If KubeArmor pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors that may indicate the cause of the crash.
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace kubearmor
```

troubleshooting.title: KubeArmor not enforcing policies
troubleshooting.description:
If KubeArmor is not enforcing the expected policies, verify that the policies are correctly defined and applied. Check the status of the KubeArmor policies.
```bash
kubectl get kubearmorpolicies --namespace kubearmor
```

troubleshooting.title: RBAC permissions issues
troubleshooting.description:
If KubeArmor cannot access certain resources, ensure that the RBAC roles and bindings are correctly configured. Check the role and binding.
```bash
kubectl get clusterrole kubearmor-role
kubectl get clusterrolebinding kubearmor-role-binding
```

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing OOMKilled
troubleshooting.description:
If the KubeArmor pods are being terminated due to OOMKilled status, consider adjusting the resource limits in the deployment. Check the current resource usage.
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> --namespace kubearmor
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher and have Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
